title: Televangelist Jesse Duplantis Asks Followers to Buy Him a Fourth Private Jet Because Jesus Wouldn't Ride a Donkey
teaser: Louisiana televangelist Jesse Duplantis, already the owner of three private jets, broadcast a fundraising appeal in May 2018 asking his followers to collectively purchase him a $54 million Dassault Falcon 7X. His theological justification: Jesus, were He alive today, would not be caught dead on a donkey.
body:
There is a verse in the Gospel of Matthew in which Jesus instructs a rich young ruler to sell his possessions and give to the poor. Jesse Duplantis, founder of Jesse Duplantis Ministries in Destrehan, Louisiana, appears to have read that passage as an instruction running exclusively in one direction. On May 28, 2018, Duplantis published a video appeal asking his followers to do the selling, so that he could do the acquiring, specifically a $54 million private jet. The rich young ruler, one assumes, would have found this arrangement more palatable.

The aircraft in question was a Dassault Falcon 7X, a long-range intercontinental business jet with a list price of approximately $54 million. Duplantis framed the purchase not as personal luxury but as a ministry necessity. The plane, he argued, would allow him to spread the gospel more efficiently than any commercial airline could manage. Speed of evangelism, apparently, scales linearly with cabin square footage.

As reported by the Washington Post, Duplantis was not starting from zero in the aviation department. At the time of his fundraising appeal, he already owned three private jets. The fourth, he explained, was simply the logical next step in a ministry that requires a man to be airborne with some regularity.

The theological centerpiece of Duplantis's appeal was a claim about what Jesus would choose to fly today. His argument, rendered plainly, was that Jesus traveled by donkey because donkeys were the available technology of the era. By this reasoning, a modern Jesus would obviously upgrade. This is a coherent enough line of thought, provided one is willing to set aside the bulk of Jesus's actual recorded teachings on wealth, humility, and material attachment.

Duplantis has not been shy about his affection for private aviation, nor about soliciting donor funds to support it. Prosperity gospel theology, which undergirds much of Duplantis's ministry, holds that material wealth is a sign of divine blessing rather than a spiritual hazard. Under this framework, a fourth private jet is not a scandal; it is a testimony. The congregation is invited to participate in the testimony by writing a check.

The mechanics of the fundraising request are worth examining on their own terms. Duplantis did not ask a single wealthy donor, a foundation, or a corporate partner to fund the aircraft. He asked his general audience, the same audience that receives his televised teachings, to collectively cover the $54 million price tag. This is a population that, statistically, does not own one private jet, let alone three. The ask represents a significant transfer of financial resources in a direction that runs opposite to what most people understand charitable giving to accomplish.

Duplantis did acknowledge, during the appeal, that the request was unusual. He reportedly told his audience that he had been hesitant to make the ask, framing it as an act of obedience to a divine instruction he had received. This framing, in which a financial solicitation is presented as an act of spiritual submission rather than personal desire, is a well-documented feature of high-dollar ministry fundraising. It positions the donor's contribution as obedience as well, which reduces the likelihood of scrutiny.

The incident did not occur in a vacuum. Duplantis had previously come under public attention for his lifestyle and ministry finances. A 2007 Senate Finance Committee investigation led by Senator Chuck Grassley examined several high-profile televangelists, though Duplantis was not among the six ministries directly targeted in that inquiry. The broader scrutiny of televangelist financial practices had been a matter of public and congressional concern well before his 2018 appeal.

The public response to the video was immediate and largely unfavorable outside of Duplantis's existing supporter base. Journalists, commentators, and critics across the ideological spectrum found the spectacle difficult to frame sympathetically. A man with three private jets asking followers with none to fund a fourth is, as a narrative, resistant to spin. Duplantis's team did not appear to substantially walk back the appeal in the days following the coverage.

It bears noting that private jet ownership is not, by itself, illegal for a religious nonprofit. Churches and ministries in the United States benefit from broad tax exemptions and are subject to less financial disclosure than secular nonprofits of comparable size. This legal architecture means that donor contributions to ministry jet funds flow through an institution that pays no federal income tax and files no public financial return. The arrangement is, at minimum, worth understanding before writing a check.

The specific aircraft Duplantis requested, the Falcon 7X, is capable of flying from the continental United States to Europe or parts of Asia without refueling. This is, objectively, more range than most evangelical preaching circuits require. Whether the gospel travels meaningfully faster at 51,000 feet than at 35,000 feet has not been the subject of peer-reviewed study, though Duplantis appears confident in the affirmative. The donkeys of first-century Judea were presumably unavailable for comment.

The incident crystallizes a tension that sits at the center of prosperity gospel ministry and its relationship to donor trust. Followers who give to a ministry operating three private jets are not, in any conventional sense, giving to the poor. They are subsidizing an institutional infrastructure that places its founder in a class of material comfort far removed from any apostolic precedent. Whether that constitutes a betrayal of faith depends entirely on which faith one is measuring against, and Jesse Duplantis has been quite deliberate about defining his own terms.
